coredata.dc:title: An injectable chitosan-based hydrogel incorporating carbon dots with dual enzyme-mimic activities for synergistically treatment of bacteria infected wounds

coredata.dc:description: Bacterial infections pose a serious threat to human health, and the emergence of superbugs and the growing antibiotic resistance phenomenon have made the development of novel antimicrobial products. In this paper, an ultrasmall Cu, N co-doped carbon dots (CDs-Cu-N) with excellent peroxidase mimic activity and enhanced catalase mimic activity was successfully prepared and anchored to an injectable chitosan (CS)-based hybrid hydrogel. As expected, the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel maintains the excellent enzyme-mimicking properties of CDs-Cu-N and shows superior antibacterial property, which has been proven to effectively promote the healing of S. aureus-infected wounds with good biocompatibility. Benefitting from the dual-enzyme-mimic activity of CDs-Cu-N, the hybrid hydrogel not only can catalyze the generation of highly toxic ROS from low concentration of H2O2 to inhibit the bacterial infections, but also can significantly promote the wound tissue repair and regeneration by improving the anoxic microenvironment and promoting neovascularization. In addition, this hybrid hydrogel also possessed excellent injectability and moldability. It can adapt to various the irregular shapes of acute wounds, maintaining a moist and safe microenvironment while prolonging the action time of nanozyme on wounds, thus promoting wound healing. This injectable hybrid hydrogel shows great potential applications in the field of wound infection management.

head.ce:title: An injectable chitosan-based hydrogel incorporating carbon dots with dual enzyme-mimic activities for synergistically treatment of bacteria infected wounds

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule Chemistry of the Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry & Materials Science, Northwest University, Xi’an 710069, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule Chemistry of the Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry & Materials Science, Northwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule Chemistry of the Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry & Materials Science, Northwest University, Xi’an 710069, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bacterial infections pose a serious threat to human health, and the emergence of superbugs and the growing antibiotic resistance phenomenon have made the development of novel antimicrobial products. In this paper, an ultrasmall Cu, N co-doped carbon dots (CDs-Cu-N) with excellent peroxidase mimic activity and enhanced catalase mimic activity was successfully prepared and anchored to an injectable chitosan (CS)-based hybrid hydrogel. As expected, the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel maintains the excellent enzyme-mimicking properties of CDs-Cu-N and shows superior antibacterial property, which has been proven to effectively promote the healing of S. aureus-infected wounds with good biocompatibility. Benefitting from the dual-enzyme-mimic activity of CDs-Cu-N, the hybrid hydrogel not only can catalyze the generation of highly toxic ROS from low concentration of H2O2 to inhibit the bacterial infections, but also can significantly promote the wound tissue repair and regeneration by improving the anoxic microenvironment and promoting neovascularization. In addition, this hybrid hydrogel also possessed excellent injectability and moldability. It can adapt to various the irregular shapes of acute wounds, maintaining a moist and safe microenvironment while prolonging the action time of nanozyme on wounds, thus promoting wound healing. This injectable hybrid hydrogel shows great potential applications in the field of wound infection management.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Microorganisms are present in all parts of the human skin and play a vital role in maintaining human health by not causing infections under normal circumstances. When the integrity of the skin is compromised or diseased, it becomes susceptible to infection by microorganisms such as bacteria, which can lead to inflammation and cause severe tissue and cellular damage, further impeding the healing process and even posing a serious threat to human life [1]. It was not until the spread of antibiotics that the trend of bacterial infections was effectively controlled. However, the overuse and misuse of antibiotics has led to the emergence of drug-resistant strains of bacteria, posing a great threat to public health and the environment [2]. It is imperative to develop alternative antimicrobial strategies and new antimicrobials to alleviate the generation of new bacterial resistance. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) is an effective substance for the organism's defense against pathogens. For example, after phagocytosis of bacteria by neutrophils of the human immune system, oxidases and peroxidases in lysosomes can catalyze the production of highly toxic ROS to destroy bacterial cell membranes, proteins or nucleic acids, thus killing the phagocytized bacteria [3]. Therefore, it is a promising sterilization strategy to use enzyme-catalyzed reactions to mimic this natural self-defense system of cells and generate ROS to attack and damage bacteria. However, the limitations of natural enzymes, including poor stability, susceptibility to inactivation, and difficulty in large-scale preparation, have greatly restricted their use in practical antibacterial applications.

Nanozymes, as a new generation of artificial enzymes, also have the ability to regulate the level of ROS, thus becoming an ideal choice in the antibacterial field [4–7]. They have similar enzymatic reaction kinetics and catalytic mechanisms as natural enzymes, but have better stability and environmental tolerance than natural enzymes [8]. So far, a variety of nanozymes with excellent antibacterial performance have been designed and synthesized, such as metal nanomaterials [9–12], carbon-based nanomaterials [13–16], transition metal oxides/sulfides [17–21], and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [22–24]. However, a number of problems associated with their practical application, such as complex synthesis procedures and potential toxicity to normal cells or tissues, still hinder their widespread application. Therefore, it is still a challenge to develop a biocompatible nanozyme with excellent and broad-spectrum antibacterial properties.

Recently, carbon dots (CDs), as a class within carbon based nanozymes, have attracted wider attention in antibacterial applications due to their high antibacterial activity, excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity [25,26]. The ultrasmall size (below 10 nm) and the abundance of functional groups on their surfaces make CDs enter or bind bacteria more easily or firmly, thus providing them with a unique advantage in antimicrobial properties [27–29]. However, the common antibacterial mechanism of CDs is depended on the ROS-induced oxidative stress by external photostimulation, and the practical applications are largely restricted by its experimental conditions and equipment limitation [30]. How to improve the inherent catalytic efficiency of CDs and endow them with more abundant functions is worth exploring. The strategy of the heteroatom doping has been proven to be an effective method [31]. Transition metal ions, N atom and S atom are likely to modulate the electronic structure and physicochemical properties of CDs, which would provide more active sites and better electron transfer efficiency to the doped CDs [32–37]. In nature, copper is an essential trace element with a variable valence state that participates in several electron transfer and redox reactions during biological metabolism. Cu doping has also been shown to be effective in enhancing the enzyme-mimic catalytic performance of carbon-based nanomaterials [34,38–41].

In this paper, an ultrasmall Cu, N co-doped carbon dots (CDs-Cu-N) with excellent peroxidase-mimic enzyme activity and enhanced catalase mimic activity was successfully prepared. The incorporation of Cu can greatly increase the active sites by coordinating with N and enriching carboxyl active groups in CDs, thereby endowing CDs-Cu-N with better enzyme mimicking properties. In order to prolong the duration of nanozyme action, we further anchored CDs-Cu-N into an injectable multifunctional hydrogel ( Scheme 1). Chitosan (CS) is an ideal substrate for the development of advanced bioactive hydrogel due to its good biocompatibility and biodegradability [42]. The polycationic character and the abundance of reactive functional groups (including hydroxyl and amine groups) give chitosan the potential to interact with the oxygenated surface functionalities in the CDs-Cu-N, thus enabling CDs-Cu-N to be well immobilized in hydrogel. Moreover, the acid hydrogel condition is also favorable to for the maintenance of the catalytic activity of the CDs-Cu-N nanozyme. As expected, the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel also maintains the excellent enzyme-mimicking properties of CDs-Cu-N and can effectively promote infected wound healing with good biocompatibility. The fabricated the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel can be used as a novel and effective repairing material with great application potential in wound healing.
Reagents and instrumentation were listed in the Supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs-Cu-N and hybrid hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: CDs-Cu-N was prepared as follows: 100 mL of solution containing EDTA-2Na∙2H2O (0.05 mol) and 100 mL of solution containing Cu(NO3)2∙3H2O (0.05 mol) were mixed thoroughly under stirring, and the precipitate was collected by filtration after 6 h of standing. The precipitate was washed with deionized water, dried and then was calcined at 300 °C for 2 h (heating rate of 5 °C/min) under nitrogen atmosphere. The obtained black product was dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water and sonicated for 1 h. The supernatant was collected by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm/min. Next, the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane to remove impurities and dialyzed in a 1000 kDa dialysis bag for 24 h. Finally, the dialyzed solution was freeze-dried to obtain CDs-Cu-N powder and stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃ under dark conditions. Meanwhile, CDs without doping Cu were synthesized by the same method using only EDTA-2Na as precursor.

60 mg of chitosan was dissolved in 1950 µL of 1% acetic acid solution containing 125 μg/mL CDs-Cu-N, and heating and stirring were used to dissolve the chitosan. After the solution cooled slightly, 50 µL of 40 mM H2O2 was added and stirred well to obtain a hybridized hydrogel. The hydrogels were then washed with water for several times to remove unreacted monomers, then they were kept in a brown reagent bottle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of mimic enzyme activity of CDs-Cu-N
ce:section.ce:para: The catalyzing activity of peroxidase-mimic enzyme of CDs-Cu-N was determined by using TMB as the chromogenic substrate. 100 μL of well-dispersed CDs-Cu-N (final concentration of 50 μg/mL), 100 μL of H2O2 (final concentration of 5 mM), and 50 μL of TMB (final concentration of 600 μM) were added to 2750 μL of 0.1 M HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH=4.5). After reacting in a water bath at 30 ℃ for 20 min, the absorbance change of the reaction system was record UV–vis spectra.

The catalase-mimic activity of CDs-Cu-N was determined using a dissolved oxygen analyzer to monitor the O2 production in the reaction solution. The O2 content was detected within 20 min by adding 10 mM H2O2 and different concentrations of CDs-Cu-N (0, 50, 100, 200, 250 μg/mL) in phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH = 7.2). The reaction temperature was 25 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady state kinetic analysis of peroxidase-mimic of CDs-Cu-N
ce:section.ce:para:
Kinetic analyses were performed in HAc-NaAc buffer solution (0.1 M, pH=4.5, final volume 3.0 mL). The solution contained CDs-Cu-N (50 μg/mL), TMB (600 μM) and different concentrations of H2O2 (0.5, 1, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10 mM), or CDs-Cu-N (50 μg/mL), H2O2 (5 mM) and different concentrations of TMB (200, 400, 600, 700, 800, 900, 1000 μM). All experiments were repeated at least three times. Michaelis-Menten constant (K m) and maximum initial velocity (v max) were calculated as follows [43]:

Determination of CDs-Cu-N specific activity: different concentrations of CDs-Cu-N dispersions were added to HAc-NaAc buffer (0.1 M, pH=4.5, final volume 3 mL) containing 5 mM H2O2 and 600 μM TMB. The absorbance value of oxTMB at 652 nm was measured and recorded at 10 s intervals for 1 min. The response curve of time versus absorbance was plotted and the catalytic activity value was calculated according to the enzyme activity response equation [43]:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the catalytic mechanism of CDs-Cu-N
ce:section.ce:para: Free radical burst experiment: p-benzoquinone (100 μL, 10 mM), L-Tryptophan (100 μL, 10 mM), and isopropanol (100 μL, 10 mM) were added into the above reaction system of CDs-Cu-N, respectively. The absorbance at 652 nm was measured after the reaction at 30 °C for 20 min. The free radicals generated during the catalytic process of the CDs-Cu-N were detected by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) technique. EPR experiments were performed using 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) as a trapping agent for 1O2 and 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as a trapping agent for •O2 ¯ and •OH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic antimicrobial activity studies of CDs-Cu-N and hybrid hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para:
The antimicrobial activity of CDs-Cu-N against Gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli), Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and pathogenic fungus Candida albicans (C. albicans) was evaluated by plate counting method. The experiments were conducted in following groups: (1) bacteria/fungi (control); (2) bacteria/fungi + H2O2; (3) bacteria/fungi + CDs-Cu-N (250 μg/mL); (4) bacteria/fungi + CDs-Cu-N (250 μg/mL) + H2O2; (5) bacteria/fungi + CDs-Cu-N (125 μg/mL); (6) bacteria/fungi + CDs-Cu-N (125 μg/mL)+H2O2; (7) bacteria/fungi + chitosan hydrogel; (8) bacteria/fungi + H2O2- chitosan hydrogel; (9) bacteria/fungi + CDs-Cu-N (125 μg/mL)- chitosan hydrogel; (10) bacteria/fungi + CDs-Cu-N (125 μg/mL)-H2O2-chitosan hydrogel. The final concentration of H2O2 was 1 mM. The activated bacteria solution was added into the reaction system of the above experimental groups (3 mL). After incubating at a constant temperature of 37℃ in a shaker for 1 h, the co-incubation solution was further diluted 20 times. 100 μL of the diluted co-incubation solution was spread on LB agar plates, and the plates were incubated at 37℃ incubators for 8–15 h. the hydrogel samples were mixed with diluted activated bacteria solution and directly spread on LB agar plates. The colonies were counted and statistically analyzed:

where Nt is the bacterial number of experimental plates and Nc is that of control plates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The scanning electron microscopy characterization of microbe
ce:section.ce:para: After co incubation of the CDs-Cu-N and H2O2, 1 mL of microbe suspension was taken and centrifuged at 6000 rpm/min for 10 minutes. Then wash with phosphate buffer 3 times to remove residual material. It was fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde overnight, and centrifuged at 6000 rpm/min to remove the supernatant. Subsequently, the samples were dehydrated by centrifugation with gradients of 30%, 50%, 70%, 90% and 100% ethanol in sequence. Finally, the samples were sprayed with gold and the morphological changes of each organism were observed using scanning electron microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Infectious wound animal model construction and treatment experiments
ce:section.ce:para: All animal experiments followed the relevant regulations of the National Institutes of Health and the Ethics Committee for Animal Experiments of Northwest University (NWU-AWC-20211102 M). Female Kunming mice of 25–30 g were used for animal experiments. First, a full-layer skin injury with a diameter of about 8 mm was created on the back of each mouse, and then 50 μL of S. aureus at a concentration of 3×106 CFU/mL was injected into the wound site and infected for 24 h. The infected mice were randomly divided into four groups: the control group (HAc-NaAc buffer), the CDs-Cu-N (125 μg/mL) chitosan hydrogel, the H2O2 chitosan hydrogel and the CDs-Cu-N (125 μg/mL)-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel group. About 100 μL of hybrid hydrogel was injected on the traumas of the corresponding groups, and the hybrid hydrogel was administered every 12 h. Wound healing was observed and recorded daily, and the wound area was statistically analyzed using Image J. At the end of 12 days of treatment, the mice were executed to collect the wound skin tissues and major organs, which were fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde solution and then paraffin-embedded. Histological and pathological analyses were performed by H&E staining to fully assess the quality of healing in bacterial infected wounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methods of statistical analysis of data
ce:section.ce:para: Unless otherwise stated, each test in this thesis was repeated at least three times and data are presented as average ± relative standard deviation. Data were analyzed for significant differences using the t-test, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ns means not significant.

The CDs-Cu-N were prepared by a facile pyrolysis of the precipitates resulting from coordination reaction of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA) with copper ion. The synthetic raw material EDTA is not only a promising source of carbon and nitrogen, but also a favorable complexant that can complex with copper ions and immobilize them on CDs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images showed that the prepared CDs-Cu-N had a uniformly dispersed spherical morphology ( Fig. 1A) with an average particle size of 2.772 nm (Fig. 1B). This ultra-small size will facilitate crossing the cellular physiological barrier and promote cellular uptake [44]. The lattice spacing in HR-TEM was 0.21 nm, corresponding to the lattice plane of the (100) face of graphitic carbon, which was consistent with the CDs in the previous reports [45]. The XRD spectra of CDs-Cu-N revealed a wide reflection peak at the center at 25°, which was also attributed to typical vibrations of graphitic carbon (Fig. 1C) [46]. Fourier infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was further compared to analyze the structures of CDs-Cu-N and CDs without Cu (Fig. 1D). It can be found that both of them showed stretching vibration peaks belonging to OH/NH, CO and CN near 3436, 1637 and 1401 cm-1, respectively [47]. Meanwhile, there was a new peak in the FT-IR spectrum of CDs-Cu-N at about 1047 cm-1, which was caused by the stretching vibration of Cu-N [48], further confirming the successful formation of Cu coordination compound in the synthesis of CDs-Cu-N.

Additionally, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) technology was further used to analyze the elemental composition of CDs-Cu-N’s surface. As shown in Fig. 1E, it was found that the contents of C, O, N and Cu were 74.00%, 14.19%, 11.68% and 0.13%, respectively. The N content was significantly higher compared to CDs without copper doping (Fig. S1A). The high-resolution C1s spectrum can be divided into three peaks located at 284.8, 285.7, and 287.1 eV, which were associated with C-C/CC, C-O/C-N, and CO, respectively (Fig. 1F) [49]. Three peaks at 531.1, 532.1, and 533.8 eV can be observed in the high-resolution O 1 s spectra, and were associated with CO, C-O, and O-CO, respectively (Fig. 1G) [50]. However, in the high-resolution O 1 s spectra of CDs (Fig. S1C), we did not obtain obvious C-O and O-CO peaks, indicating that the introduction of Cu favored the enrichment of carboxyl groups on the CDs surface. The high-resolution N1s spectrum was deconvoluted into three peaks belonging to pyridinic-N (398.6 eV), pyrolic-N (399.8 eV) and graphitc-N (401.7 eV) (Fig. 1H) [51]. The doping of N was expected to combine with the carbon-based material to form external defects and active sites, thus enhancing the overall catalytic activity of the CDs[52]. In addition, the Cu 2p spectra (Fig. 1I) also showed the presence of Cu+ and Cu2+.The peaks at 932.0 and 952.0 eV corresponded to the characteristics of Cu+, while the peaks at 933.9 and 954.5 eV were attributed to the characteristics Cu2+ [34]. All the above results validated the successful doping of Cu and N in CDs.

The catalase-mimic activity the prepared CDs-Cu-N was preliminarily evaluated by monitoring the O2-generating capacity of CDs-Cu-N in 10 mM H2O2. As shown in Fig. 2A, compared with CDs, the introduction of Cu significantly enhanced the catalase-mimic activity of CDs-Cu-N. The production of dissolved oxygen in the reaction solution steadily increased with the passage of time and the increase of CDs-Cu-N concentration. Even in weakly acidic environments, CDs-Cu-N still exhibited certain catalase-mimic activity (Fig. S2). The peroxidase-mimic activities of CDs-Cu-N was further investigated by using TMB as chromogenic subtract. As depicted in Fig. 2B, after incubating with CDs-Cu-N, the colorless solutions of TMB-H2O2 changed to blue gradually, and an obvious corresponding characteristic peak appeared at 652 nm can be observed in the UV–vis absorption spectra. In contrast, there was neither color change nor absorbance enhancement at 652 nm of CDs-Cu-N with TMB or H2O2, while the TMB-H2O2 system incubating with CDs also did not feature significant chromogenic reaction. These results indicated that CDs-Cu-N possessed peroxidase mimetic catalytic activity, and the doping of Cu was the main active site for CDs-Cu-N with peroxidase mimic catalytic function. In addition, we also performed titration analysis of surface carboxylic acids of CDs-Cu-N and CDs with NaOH [53]. The consumption of NaOH (1 mM) was found to be 35.70 and 20.20 mL for 100.00 mL of 10 μg/mL CDs-Cu-N and CDs, respectively, which indicated that CDs-Cu-N possess significantly more carboxyl groups than CDs. The carboxylate-rich groups on the surface of CDs-Cu-N have been proved as an effective binding site for the substrate to trap H2O2, which would reduce spatial hindrance and promote electron transfer from CDs-Cu-N to H2O2, thereby enhancing the peroxidase mimic activity of CDs-Cu-N [54].

Preliminary studies were also performed that the catalyst concentration, substrate concentration pH, and temperature all have an impact on the catalytic activity of CDs-Cu-N. As shown in Fig. 2C, the absorbance reached its maximum value in the acidic condition and the temperature of 30 ℃. And the absorbance increased with reaction time and the concentration of catalyst (Fig. 2D). CDs-Cu-N (50 μg/mL) was adopted in the following experiments as consideration of the catalytic activity of CDs-Cu-N at 50 μg/mL was about 91.2% of that at 60 μg/mL. In order to systematically study the peroxidase mimic activity of CDs-Cu-N, the steady-state kinetic analysis was also carried out under optimal experimental conditions with TMB and H2O2 as substrates. As can be seen in Fig. 2E and F, the catalytic velocity of CDs-Cu-N gradually increased with the increase of TMB concentration or H2O2 concentration, and then levelled off. The CDs-Cu-N mimetic enzyme reaction conformed to the typical Michaelis-Menten mechanism. The K m was calculated to be 0.12 mM and 1.0 mM for TMB and H2O2, respectively. Compared with other CDs based peroxidase mimic enzymes (Table S1), it had a comparable or stronger affinity for the substrates. The specific activity (SA) of CDs-Cu-N as mimic peroxidase was calculated as 0.32 U/mg (Fig. S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms for peroxidase-mimetic activity of CDs-Cu-N
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to clarify the mechanism of the peroxidase mimic activity of CDs-Cu-N, the following experiments were performed. The free radical bursting experiments was firstly used to examine whether there was a production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) during the catalysis process. p-benzoquinone (p-BQ), isopropanol (IPA), and L-tryptophan (TRP) were selected as ROS scavengers for scavenging •O2ˉ, •OH, and 1O2, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3A, the peroxidase-mimic activity of CDs-Cu-N was significantly reduced by the introduction of p-BQ and TRP, whereas the peroxidase- mimetic activity of CDs-Cu-N was almost unchanged in the presence of IPA. These results suggested that CDs-Cu-N would interact with H2O2 to produce •O2ˉand 1O2, thus exhibiting the peroxidase mimetic activity. In addition, the EPR was further tested using DMPO and TEMP as spin trapping agents. As shown in Fig. 3B and C, the TEMP-1O2 triple peak signal (1:1:1) was observed in the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2 system with the addition of TEMP, indicating that 1O2 was generated in the catalyzed process of CDs-Cu-N-H2O2 system. No DMPO-OH peaks (1:2:2:1) was detected in the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2 system, which was consistent with the free radical bursting experiments. However, it was found that a strong DMPO-•O2ˉsignal with a quadruple peak of 1:1:1:1 appeared after 1 min. The appearance of the DMPO-•O2ˉsignal might be ascribed to the fast transformation of •OH to •O2ˉon the active sites of the CDs-Cu-N composites. The high-resolution Cu 2p spectrum (Fig. 1I) has showed that the Cu element in the CDs-Cu-N mainly existed in the form of Cu+ and Cu2+, which is also widely used as a catalyst for Fenton-like reaction. They can catalyze H2O2 to produce •OH and •O2ˉ according to the following reactions (Eqs. (3-1)–(3-5)) [55]. Then the recombination of •O2ˉ resulted in the production of 1O2 (Eq. (3-6)) [56].

H2O2 is commonly used as an antimicrobial agent in clinical practice, but it can effectively scavenge bacteria only at higher concentrations (166 mM-1.0 M), which will inevitably produce side effects on healthy tissues. According to previous reports [7], nanomaterials with peroxidase-mimic activity can efficiently catalyze the generation of more toxic ROS from low concentrations of H2O2, thereby more effectively clearing pathogenic bacteria. Based on the excellent peroxidase-mimic catalytic activity of CDs-Cu-N, the antimicrobial ability of CDs-Cu-N-H2O2 system was further investigated through a plate count method. Three common pathogenic microbe, gram-negative (E. coli), gram-positive (S. aureus) bacteria, and pathogenic fungus (C. albicans), were employed to evaluate the broad-spectrum antimicrobial ability of the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2 system. As shown in Fig. 4A, compared with the control group, the single H2O2 group CDs-Cu-N group, the growth and proliferation of E. coli, S. aureus and C. albicans were significantly inhibited after one hour of co-incubation with CDs-Cu-N-H2O2 solution. The CDs-Cu-N-H2O2 group exhibited the optimal antibacterial performance, with colony numbers of S. aureus, E. coli and C. albicans reduced by 5.4, 5.2, 5.1 orders of magnitude, respectively, equivalent to bacterial killing rates of >99.999%. In order to explore the relationship between the antimicrobial activity and concentration of CDs-Cu-N, the three microbes were incubated with gradient concentration of CDs-Cu-N in the presence of H2O2. As shown in Fig. S4, the viability of E. coli, S. aureus and C. albicans decreased with increasing CDs-Cu-N concentration. The MIC value of 62.5 μg/mL and MBC value of 125 μg/mL were obtained.

To further characterize the antimicrobial behavior of CDs-Cu-N-H2O2 system, the morphology changes of E. coli, S. aureus and C. albicans were investigated by the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images. As demonstrated in Fig. 4B, the microbe in control group remained typically spherical or rod-shaped with an intact and smooth surface. In contrast, microbe treated with CDs-Cu-N-H2O2 hybrid hydrogel exhibited severe membrane deformations such as cell membrane contraction, denaturation and rupture, leading to cytoplasmic loss. We speculated that the effective antimicrobial function of CDs-Cu-N-H2O2 hybrid hydrogel was also related to the formation of ROS. As proved by EPR and free radical bursting experiments before, the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2 system could produce •O2ˉand 1O2 by the catalytic reaction. The generated ROS possesses strong oxidative effects and will diffuse into the microbial cells, destroying the cell membrane structure and cellular components (including DNA and proteins), thus interfering with the physiological activities of the microbe and achieving inactivation effects. Moreover, copper atoms from CDs-Cu-N have also been reported to have a strong affinity for peptidoglycan and lipopolysaccharide, which are the main markers of in the bacteria cell wall [34,57,58]. The tight coordination and the ultra-small size will facilitate the immobilization of CDs-Cu-N on surface of microbe, thereby limiting the diffusion of ROS and enhancing the antimicrobial effect of the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2 system.

Considering the further biomedical applications, the biocompatibility of the newly prepared material CDs-Cu-N was evaluated by investigating its hemocompatibility and cytocompatibility. As shown in Fig. S5A, after 2 h of co-incubation of red blood cells with CDs-Cu-N at a concentration of 500 μg/mL, the supernatant was still relatively clear and no obvious hemolysis of hemoglobin was observed. The detection value of hemolysis rate was 3.6%, which was in line with the national safety standard index (<5.0%). Apparently, the high concentration of CDs-Cu-N had little toxic effect on red blood cells and showed good hemocompatibility. Meanwhile, the CCK-8 kit assay was further adopted to investigate the cytotoxicity of CDs-Cu-N in L929 cells. Quantitative statistical analysis of L929 cell viability was monitored after co-culturing with different concentrations of CDs-Cu-N for 24 h. As displayed in Fig. S5B, it was found that cells co-culturing with CDs-Cu-N did not show significant toxicity when the CDs-Cu-N concentration was increased from 0 to 250 μg/mL. And compared to the control, all concentrations of CDs-Cu-N showed significant proliferation over 24 h with normal cell morphology (Fig. S5C). All these results indicated that the prepared CDs-Cu-N had good biosafety.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Prepare and characterization of CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: In vitro antimicrobial experiments and good biocompatibility have showed that the CDs-Cu-N has great potential for application in the field of infected wound repair. In order to effectively increase the duration of the CDs-Cu-N nanozyme on infected wounds, we further prepared a CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel by adding the natural polymer chitosan. Considering that the CDs-Cu-N possessed excellent peroxidase mimetic activity under acidic conditions, we preferably chose to prepare CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel in acetic acid system. The CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel was produced by hydrolysis and acylation of chitosan and acetic acid. The strong cationic activity of chitosan could interact with CDs-Cu-N with carboxyl groups and incorporated the CDs-Cu-N well inside the networking of hydrogel. The morphology of the hydrogel was shown in Fig. 5A. The hybrid hydrogel consists mainly of 2D lamellar membranes interwoven to form separate 3D structures, and CDs-Cu-N were uniformly distributed in the hydrogel. Fig. 5B visualized the flow ability of the hybrid hydrogel in the gelatinized state. Unlike the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2 liquid, no significant flow was observed in the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel after standing 3 minutes, confirming the successful construction of the hydrogel. In addition, the hybrid hydrogel also has good injectable ability, which allows to adjust different shapes through the syringe without clogging (Fig. 5C). And the hybrid hydrogel can be firmly adhered to human skin tissues. The adjustable adaptability and formability of hydrogel allow flexibility in matching the wounds accurately according to the shape of the injured area. Moreover, CDs-Cu-N still exhibits excellent peroxidase mimetic catalytic activity in hybrid hydrogel system (Fig. 5D), which exhibited almost 100% antimicrobial effect against E. coli, S. aureus and C. albicans (Fig. S6), and can be used as an effective treatment for the bacteria and does kill it off.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo evaluation of infected wound healing
ce:section.ce:para: A whole skin wound model of S. aureus infection was constructed to investigate the ability of the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel to promote the healing of infected wounds ( Fig. 6A). An infectious wound model of S. aureus with a diameter of approximately 8 mm was successfully established on the back of Kunming female mice, and then the animals were randomly divided into four groups. Subsequently, the wounds were injected twice a day using about 100 μL of each group's solution, and photographs of the wound site were taken periodically to analyze the changes in wound size and wound healing. Fig. 6B-C presented the photographs of the wounds during the healing period. It can be noticed that the wounds of all groups started to close on the 3rd day after surgery. At 9 days of wound treatment, the wound size of the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel group was significantly smaller than the other three groups. And when the wound was treated for 12 days, the wound defect in the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel group had healed, while the wounds in the other groups still maintained a certain unhealed area, indicating that CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel could significantly accelerate the healing of infected wounds. Quantitative analysis of the wound healing area was given in Fig. 6D. The average wound healing mouse of the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel group reached 99.64%, whereas the average healing mice of the control group, the CDs-Cu-N-CS hydrogel group and the H2O2-CS hydrogel group were 80.82%, 88.76% and 90.06%, respectively. Throughout the healing process, the wound healing mouse of the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel group was significantly faster than that of the remaining three groups, showing excellent pro-infectious wound healing ability. Moreover, we also analyzed the antibacterial effect of CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel in vivo by collecting local bacteria from all groups of wounds (Fig. S7). It was found that the number of bacteria in wounds treated by CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel for three days was significantly less than that of other groups, indicating this hybrid hydrogel structure can effectively protect the wound from secondary infection by surrounding bacteria. Besides, the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel can also absorb the spillage from the wounds, maintaining a moist and safe microenvironment while prolonging the action time of nanozyme on wounds, thus promoting wound healing.

To further assess the recovery of infected wounds after CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel treatment, H&E staining was performed on skin tissues from the original wound area. Fig. 7A displayed the results of H&E staining of wound tissue sections of each group on day 12 after surgery. As can be seen, the basic structure of regenerated skin tissue could already be observed in the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel group on day 12, with the thickness of the epithelial layer obviously thinner than that of the other three groups. There were many newborn hair follicles, distributed blood vessels and a large number of fibroblasts which was very close to the structure of normal skin tissue. In contrast, the inflammatory factors were still present in the skin tissues of the control and CDs-Cu-N hydrogel group; the H2O2 hydrogel group had only a basic epithelial structure with a small number of neonatal hair follicles and blood vessels. The results of the number of hair follicles (Fig. 7C) also further demonstrated that CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel has the ability to significantly accelerate tissue remodeling in infected wounds.

Meanwhile, two typical pro-inflammatory cytokines, tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α) and interleukin-6 (IL-6), were further selected to assess the level of inflammatory response in wounds of different experimental groups. Fig. 7B showed the immunohistochemical analysis of tissue sections from the wound area on day 12 was performed using TNF-α and IL-6 antibodies. It could be found that there was still significant positive expression of TNF-α and IL-6 antibodies in the control, CDs-Cu-N hydrogel and H2O2 hydrogel groups, while it was much lower in the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel group. Quantitative analysis of the above results (Fig. 7D and E) showed that the positive expression of TNF-α and IL-6 in the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel group were 2.1% and 5.6%, respectively, with a significantly lower level of inflammatory response than the other groups. These results suggested that the disinfectant of CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel could inhibit bacterial infection in wounds through bactericidal action and down-regulate the pro-inflammatory factors TNF-α and IL-6, thereby shortening the inflammatory period to accelerate the healing of infected wounds.

In addition, angiogenesis is also an essential part in the wound healing process, as it helps promote cell proliferation and matrix remodeling at the wound site by providing oxygen and nutrients. Therefore, we also investigated the markers of blood vessels, vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), α-smooth muscle actin (α-SMA) and platelet endothelial cell adhesion molecule-1(CD31) underwent immunohistochemical testing to evaluate the formation of new blood vessels at the wound site [59–61]. As shown in Fig. 7B, the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel group showed more pronounced red and green fluorescent signals compared with the control, merely CDs-Cu-N hydrogel group and H2O2 hydrogel group. And the quantitative analysis results (Fig. 7F-H) also showed that the three biomarkers in the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel group were significantly higher than those in the other groups, indicating that the vascular density of the wound tissue in the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel group was significantly higher than that in the other groups. We speculate that the well-developed vascular density in the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel is attributed to the catalase-mimicking activity of CDs-Cu-N. The hybrid hydrogel can decompose H2O2 into O2 via its catalase-mimicking activity, thus ensuring the supply of oxygen in the wound microenvironment, further promoting angiogenesis and accelerating wound healing. Subsequent cell migration experiments also confirmed that the presence of CDs-Cu-H2O2 had a promoting effect on cell proliferation (Fig. S8).

Moreover, to further evaluate the in vivo biosafety of the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel system, we also performed tissue sections and H&E staining analyses on the major organs of heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney of mice. As shown in Fig. S9, no histopathological abnormalities were observed in all groups of treated mice, and there was no difference in body weight and organ weights. These results indicated that the adverse effects of CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel involved in wound healing were negligible, and CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel mediated treatments were biologically safe.

In conclusion, we have rationally designed a kind of Cu, N co-doped CDs with excellent peroxidase mimic activity and enhanced catalase mimic activity. The incorporation of Cu can greatly increase the active sites by coordinating with N and enriching carboxyl active groups in CDs, thereby endowing CDs-Cu-N with better enzyme mimicking properties. In order to better immobilize CDs-Cu-N and to effectively increase the duration of the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2 system, we further anchored them with chitosan to a multifunctional hybrid hydrogel. As expected, the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel maintains the excellent enzyme-mimicking properties and superior antimicrobial properties of CDs-Cu-N, which has also shown great potential in promoting wound healing caused by bacterial infections. In addition, the CDs-Cu-N-H2O2-CS hybrid hydrogel also exhibits excellent biocompatibility and will not cause substantial inflammation or pathological damage to normal tissues, which suggests that they have a wide range of applied prospects to fields such as antimicrobial, pharmaceutical.
